coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent and pH sensitive nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) as a fluorescent sensor for the efficient detection of Cr (VI) ions in aqueous media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Jasjot
Sharma, Shelja
Mehta, Surinder Kumar
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are contemporary class of fluorescent materials that has emerged recently and have gathered increasing attention due to its excellent properties as compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. CDs have lucrative benefits of less toxicity, biocompatibility, eco friendliness, tunable fluorescence, high chemical and photostability, effortless synthesis routes and uncomplicated surface modifications and functionalization. In the present work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal process using l-ascorbic acid and ethylene diamine as precursors. The as-prepared NCDs were hydrophilic in nature and could remain stable for several weeks. NCDs displayed bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation and also exhibited an extensive range of emission spectra in the visible region to infra-red region based upon the excitation wavelength. NCDs possessed quasi-spherical morphology and high density growth. NCDs were further utilized as nanoprobes for the pH sensing and proficient sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions present in aqueous phase. Under augmented modifications and conditions, the photoluminescence intensity of NCDs against various micromolar concentration of chromium (VI) ions presented a linear relationship, as per Stern–Volmer equation. The calibration curve was found to be linear in the range of 0–4 μM and from the slope of the linear curve, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.598 nM. The Stern-Volmer calibration curve was also plotted against different temperatures, verifying static quenching mechanism. Therefore, the as synthesized NCDs can be successfully demonstrated for the efficient pH sensing and the detection of Cr (VI) ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensing
Metal ions
Fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)
Effect of ions on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs
Sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions
Influence of metal ions on the NCDs
Effect of Cr (VI) ions on the fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) FESEM images, (c) EDS and (d) HRTEM of fluorescent NCDs.
(a-d) Mapping of NCDs, C, O and N respectively.
Effect of (a) excitation wavelength and (b) pH on fluorescence intensity of NCDs. Upconversion PL spectra at different excitation wavelength from (c) 500–720 nm and (d) 710 nm–870 nm.
Effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of NCDs.

(a) Selective fluorescence sensing of Cr(VI) ions using fluorescent NCDs with respect to different cations and anions (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs in the presence of different molar amounts of Cr(VI) ions at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.
Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of NCDs by Cr6+ ions at different temperatures (a) 5 °C (278 K), (b) 15 °C (288 K) and (c) 30 °C (303 K).
Stern-Volmer constant of interaction between NCDs and Cr(VI) at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent and pH sensitive nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) as a fluorescent sensor for the efficient detection of Cr (VI) ions in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S.S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S.S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dr. S.S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are contemporary class of fluorescent materials that has emerged recently and have gathered increasing attention due to its excellent properties as compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. CDs have lucrative benefits of less toxicity, biocompatibility, eco friendliness, tunable fluorescence, high chemical and photostability, effortless synthesis routes and uncomplicated surface modifications and functionalization. In the present work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal process using l-ascorbic acid and ethylene diamine as precursors. The as-prepared NCDs were hydrophilic in nature and could remain stable for several weeks. NCDs displayed bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation and also exhibited an extensive range of emission spectra in the visible region to infra-red region based upon the excitation wavelength. NCDs possessed quasi-spherical morphology and high density growth. NCDs were further utilized as nanoprobes for the pH sensing and proficient sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions present in aqueous phase. Under augmented modifications and conditions, the photoluminescence intensity of NCDs against various micromolar concentration of chromium (VI) ions presented a linear relationship, as per Stern–Volmer equation. The calibration curve was found to be linear in the range of 0–4 μM and from the slope of the linear curve, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.598 nM. The Stern-Volmer calibration curve was also plotted against different temperatures, verifying static quenching mechanism. Therefore, the as synthesized NCDs can be successfully demonstrated for the efficient pH sensing and the detection of Cr (VI) ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the requirement of highly efficient techniques for selective and sensitive identification of toxic heavy metal ions has gained considerate attention, be indebted to their detrimental effects towards environmental pollution [1,2]. Amongst numerous heavy metal ions, peculiar concern is paid to chromium (VI) ions. Chromium has proved a boon in the era of industrialization, due to its ample usage in various industries such as electroplating, leather tanning, pigments and dyes, steel manufacturing and wood preservation. Meanwhile, the discharge of effluents from these industries containing chromium and its compounds is a bane to the environment and human health. Chromium, a pernicious toxin, is the second most abundant contaminant in the groundwater, which poses a serious threat to the aquatic life as well as to humans [3]. In the year 2015, Cr (VI) ion was found for the contamination of drinking water around the smelters in South Africa [4]. In water, chromium exhibits stability in two forms-trivalent Cr (III) and hexavalent Cr (VI). The trivalent chromium, Cr (III) ion is an important micronutrient for the proper functioning of the human body, whereas Cr (VI) ion is considered as a severe contaminant as reported by the International Agency For Research on Cancer (IARC) [5–7]. Cr (VI) and its compounds invaded our bodies through ingestion, inhalation, and contact with skin. Cr (VI) has an adverse impact on human health, causing genotoxic effects (DNA strand break, chromosomal effects, lung cancer, etc.), hemotoxicity (renal damage and neurological effects) and carcinogenicity [8,9]. According to the World Health Organization, the maximum permissible limit of 1.0 μM is considered safe for drinking water [10]. The toxicity, high mobility, and bioaccumulation of Cr (VI) compounds have raised the concern for selective and sensitive detection of Cr (VI) ions.

For many years, several methods have been adopted for the detection of Cr (VI) ion including chromatography, atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), X-ray fluorescence (XRF) [11–13] and so on. Mostly these methods serve their purpose efficiently but also, there are few drawbacks associated such as these are not cost-effective, consumes a lot of time, have complex pre- and post-treatment procedure and also, the operation and handling of sophisticated instruments is not simple, all these drawbacks restrict their widespread applicability [14]. Hence, it is extremely recommended upgrading and polish the already developed alternative methods or acquire other simplistic, quick and low-cost approaches for the proficient detection of Cr (VI) ions.

Highly fluorescent CDs, a recent member in carbon nanomaterials family, have gathered enormous concentration owing to their captivating characteristics, like exceptional chemical and photostability, variable fluorescence excitation and emission, favourable bio-compatibility, nil-toxicity and good hydrophilicity [15–18]. All these extraordinary features make CDs extensively applied in the field of bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, energy storage and photovoltaic devices [19–23]. Though CDs have potential to exhibit stupendous results in terms of their luminescence behaviour, yet the issue related to its relative low fluorescence quantum yield raises concerns among scientific community and is quite gruesome which is needed to be resolved. Notwithstanding, in the interest of improving fluorescence quantum yield, doping CDs with different heteroatoms receive great research concerns [24–28]. Since, these heteroatoms have atomic orbits and radii comparable to carbon, thereby results in the formation of a new surface state which can trap excited electrons and hence, enhance the density and improve the fluorescence quantum yield [29–32]. Owing to the fascinating features of doped CDs, it is predominantly essential to design an extremely selective, highly sensitive and easy to operate multifunctional platform for the determination of hazardous metal ions.

In the present work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been successfully synthesized by the facile hydrothermal method. The synthesized NCDs were then characterized in detail by a number of spectroscopic and analytical techniques. NCDs exhibited excellent photostability, pH and excitation wavelength dependent behaviour. Significantly, fluorescent NCDs were developed for the sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions in the aqueous phase, resulting in fluorescence quenching of NCDs.

l-Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99.0%), ethanol (C2H6O, 99.9%), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99%), ethylene diamine (C2H8N2, 99%) were bought from Merck, India. Sodium chloride (NaCl, >99.5%), strontium chloride (SrCl2, >97%) lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, >99%), silver chloride (AgCl, >99%) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2, >99%) were bought from Merck, India. Cadmium sulfate (CdSO4, >97%), copper sulfate (CuSO4, >99%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, >95%) and aluminium sulfate (Al2(SO4)3, >99%) were bought from S.D. Fine Chem. Ltd (India). Mineral acids such as HCl, HNO3, H2SO4 and HF were purchased from Merck, India. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade. Double distilled water has been used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis process, 0.5 g of ascorbic acid and 1 mL of ethylene diamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively were added to the mixture of 10 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of distilled water. The resultant mixture was kept under sonication for 10 min to form a homogeneous mixture. The reaction mixture was then transferred into the teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in an oven for 6 h at 150 °C. After the reaction got over, the autoclave was allowed to cool down naturally. Eventually a dark brown suspension was collected, indicating the successful synthesis of CDs and was further kept in an oven for 3 days at 100 °C for drying. Finally, NCDs powder was synthesized and further characterized by numerous techniques and employed to be highly fluorescent sensor for the determination of chromium (VI) ions in an aqueous phase.

The crystallinity, phase and structural analysis were investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANanalytical X'pert Pro.), with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å) at 45 kV and 40 mA in the 2θ ranging from 10° to 80°. The size and general morphologies of the prepared sample was analysed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; HITACHI, SU8010) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscope (EDX) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; TECNAI- 42200 STWIN (SEI HOLAND)). The chemical composition of the prepared sample was determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Thermo scientific Nicolet iS50) equipped with a diamond ATR at room-temperature with KBr pellet as a reference ranging from 450 to 4000 cm−1. The optical characteristics of the synthesized NCDs were analysed by room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000; 5J1-004 model) using quartz cuvette 1 cm path length. The slit width used for both excitation and emission was 5 nm.

For the fluorescence sensing of Cr (VI) ion, the stock solution of Cr (VI) was prepared from K2Cr2O7. Cr (VI) stock solution of 10−1 M was prepared and the other concentrations of Cr (VI), i.e. 10−2 M, 10−3 M, 10−4 M, 10−5 M and 10−6 M were prepared by diluting the original stock solution of 10−1 M Cr (VI). About 2 mL of NCDs solution of concentration 10 μg/mL was filled into the cuvette. The certain equivalents of Cr (VI) solution were added to the NCDs solution using micropipette. The final volume of solution remained intact as a very minute quantity of Cr (VI) was introduced into it. This mixture of solution was incubated for half a minute and then it was excited at 370 nm. The emission values for corresponding excitation values were recorded. The experimental data was fitted to Stern-Volmer equation to determine lowest detection limit and Stern-Volmer constant (KSV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ions on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of cations such as K+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Sr2+, Ag+, Na+ and anions such as F−, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2− were prepared from K2SO4, CdCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, K2Cr2O7, MgSO4, CuCl2, SrCl2, AgNO3, NaCl and HF, HCl, HNO3 and H2SO4 in distilled water of concentration 0.1 M. For metal ion detection, suspension of metal salt was mixed with NCDs solution and incubated for half a minute. After that, fluorescence emission spectra of mixture solutions were recorded at excitation wavelength of 370 nm at PMT 700 V.

The crystallinity, phase analysis and structural properties of NCDs were investigated by X-ray diffraction. Fig. 1 represents the XRD diffraction pattern of synthesized NCDs. The characteristic peak of prepared NCDs was positioned at 2θ = 21.35⁰, which might be corresponded to the d spacing of 0.42 nm along the (002) plane of graphitic structure of carbon, showing amorphous carbon. It could be clearly seen that the obtained diffraction peak was consistent with the reported literature [33]. The prominent broad diffraction peak in the XRD pattern might be due to oxygen containing functional groups distributed on basal plane and the edges of the NCDs, which resulted in increase in the basal plane distance of NCDs.

The general structure and morphology of NCDs were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Fig. 2 a and b displays FESEM images of NCDs. Fig. 2a indicated that synthesized NCDs possessed quasi-spherical morphology with even size and uniformity. There was high density growth of NCDs and due to that some agglomerations were also observed (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2d represents high resolution TEM image which indicated that the NCDs are quasi-spherical in shape with distinct lattice fringe of 0.21 nm [34]. HRTEM image and FESEM images are well-consistent with each other.

Fig. 2c illustrates the surface composition of NCDs. It could be concluded that the NCDs mainly consisted of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen as shown in Table 1 . There was no other kind of impurity indicating pure nitrogen doping into C-dots.

The elemental mapping was done to estimate the spatial distribution of elements in the C-dots. Fig. 3 indicates the uniform distribution of elements in NCDs. The successful doping of nitrogen atom in NCDs could also be seen in Fig. 3d.

The presence of functional groups on the surface of NCDs was analysed by Fourier transformation Infra-red spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 4 . FTIR spectrum of NCDs presented the following revelations; the absorption peak at 3276 cm−1 indicated the OH stretching vibration of carboxylic acid. A sharp peak at 1655 cm−1 indicated the presence of amide linkage –CONH– which demonstrated the successful doping of nitrogen into C-dots. The peaks at 2947 cm−1 and 858 cm−1 were related to the stretching and rocking band vibrations of C–H bond. The peak at 1242 cm−1 was corresponded to the stretching of phenolic C–OH group. The peaks at 1056 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 were ascribed to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of C–O–C groups [35,36].

The optical properties of NCDs were investigated by UV–vis absorbance spectrum and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 5 shows the UV–vis spectrum of NCDs. NCDs exhibited two absorption peaks at 210 nm and 285 nm. The former peak was related to π-π* electronic transition of C=C bond and the latter was ascribed to n-π* transition of carbonyl bond [37]. The NCDs aqueous solution has light yellowish colour under daylight and displays distinct blue luminescence under UV excitation (inset of Fig. 5).

PL spectra of fluorescence NCDs were recorded at different excitation wavelength (Fig. 6 a). It was observed that fluorescence behaviour of NCDs demonstrated excitation dependent phenomenon. It indicated that with the change in the value of excitation wavelength, the emission spectra also varied. It was also observed that at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm the emission peak was observed at 465 nm with maximum emission intensity. Also, with the increase in excitation wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks showed bathochromic shift but with the change in emission intensity. The intensity of emission peak started increasing from 340 nm, became maximum at 370 nm and it got decrease at 390 nm. This could be due to the presence of different sized nanoparticles in the surface energy traps of NCDs. In addition to that, it could also be stated that the smaller sized nanoparticles could excite at a lower excitation wavelength, whereas larger sized nanoparticles could excite at higher excitation wavelength [2,38]. Therefore, the optimum excitation wavelength for all the fluorescence experiments was preferred to be 370 nm.

The impact of pH on the nature of intensity of fluorescent NCDs was also studied (Fig. 6b). For this purpose, specific concentration of NCDs (0.25 μg/mL) were dispersed in double distilled water. Different pH values ranging from 4 to 11of NCDs were prepared by using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. In a typical process, specific volume (2 mL) of resultant NCDs solution of particular pH was placed in cuvette and fluorescence spectrum was obtained. It was observed that the NCDs exhibited maximum emission intensity at neutral pH = 7 with 370 nm as an excitation wavelength. At pH < 7, i.e. 4 and 6, there was decrease in fluorescence emission intensity which could be due to the induced intramolecular hydrogen bonding of carboxylic acid moieties present on the surface of NCDs. Further, at higher pH values, i.e. 9 and 11, again the fluorescence emission intensity was lower as compared to that of pH 7. The plausible reason for that could be the deprotonation of carboxylic groups moieties which started to construct negative charge over the surface of NCDs, thereby leading to the formation of anionic double layers. The presence of such anionic double layers would disrupt the origin of fluorescence phenomenon and quenched the fluorescence intensity at basic conditions [39]. Hence, pH studies revealed that the as prepared NCDs showed sensitive behaviour towards pH deviation and has promising potential as pH sensor.

Upconversion PL is the phenomenon which took place when the energy of the emitted photons is relatively greater than the energy of excited photons. Upconverted materials can absorb near infrared light (longer wavelength irradiation) and transform the irradiation or emit visible light. (shorter wavelength irradiation). In the case of NCDs, it was observed that when the solution of NCDs was excited with excitation wavelength ranging from 500 nm to 720 nm (Fig. 6c), there was gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity but without any significant shift in emission peak and the maximum emisstion was located at 453 nm, suggesting that the emission range falls in the visible region without any change in wavelength. At excitation wavelengths from 710 nm to 870 nm, NCDs displayed the emission in visible region, however, with hyperchromic shift (Fig. 6d). The trend followed by NCDs confirmed the upconversion phenomenon which could be credited to the multiphoton active process.

The fluorescence spectra of NCDs were also obtained in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl solution to investigate the ionic stability of NCDs under high ionic strength environments (Fig. 7 ). It could be seen that there was no significant change in the fluorescence spectrum of each concentration, indicating that the presence of ionic salt in the suspension of NCDs has no considerable effect on its emission intensity or in any other major appearance of peak; the spectra was found almost constant for NaCl concentration from 0.1 M to 0.5 M, indicating the high stability of NCDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of chromium (VI) ions
Influence of metal ions on the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity of prepared NCDs, the different cations and anions like K+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Cr6+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Ag+, Na+, F−, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2− were introduced into the cuvette along with NCDs and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded (Fig. 8 a). The fluorescence emission intensity of NCDs with all the cations and anions was found to be realtively less than the blank solution of NCDs, however the difference is quite insignificant for all except chromium ions. There was noteworthy and substantial quenching in fluorescence intensity in the case of Cr6+ ions as compared to the blank solution, i.e. only NCDs and other ions. From the results, it could be inferred that the NCDs served as highly efficient and selective fluorescent probes for the determination of Cr6+ ions. This might be attributed to the binding of Cr6+ ions to the surface of NCDs, which caused quenching of the fluorescence emission due to the charge transfer phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Cr (VI) ions on the fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8b displayed the effect of varied concentrations of chromium ions on the fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs. Upon the introduction of Cr6+ ions with gradual increase in concentration to the aqueous solution of NCDs of the fixed concentration, respective fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. With increase in concentration of Cr6+ ranging from 10−1 to 10−6 M, fluorescence intensity decreased gradually. This could be attributed to the binding of chromium ions over the surface of NCDs, affecting the surface states of NCDs, thereby caused transfer of charge from NCDs to Cr (VI) ions and quenched the fluorescence emission intensity magnificently. Sharma et al. reported the detection of Cr6+ ions in aqueous phase with the fluorescence quenching of spongy carbon nanoglobules (0.25 μg/mL) with LOD of 0.052 μM [33]. From the experimental results, the data was further applied to the Stern-Volmer equation as mentioned below:
Io/I = 1+ Ksv[Q]
where I0 and I are the fluorescence emission intensities of aqueous blank solution of NCDs and NCDs in the presence of chromium ions, respectively. Q is quencher's concentration, (chromium ions) and Ksv is denoted as the Stern-Volmer constant. The calibration curve (Fig. 9 a) was obtained from the plot of I0/I versus the concentration of Cr6+ and it was observed that there was linear relationship (R2 = 0.9972) between x and y axis of over the range of 0–4 μM which indicated the static quenching, leading to the formation of non-fluorescent complex. The lowest detection limit was computed to 2.598 nM from formula 3χ/m, where χ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of the linear curve and Ksv was found to be 3.29 × 105 M−1. The plausible explanation was further confirmed by performing the fluorescence sensing experiments at different set of temperatures, i.e. 5 °C, 15 °C and 30 °C, keeping other parameters at optimum conditions. The Stern-Volmer calibration curve was plotted for all the temperatures and it was observed the value of KSV was decreasing with the rise in temperature (as shown in Table 2 ), since increasing temperature tend to decrease the binding interactions that cause static quenching [41]. Hence, the results are in consistent with the static quenching mechanism.

NCDs have been successfully synthesized by facile hydrothermal treatment of ascorbic acid and ethylene diamine. The synthesized NCDs exhibited excellent water solubility, stability against ionic environment and optical properties. XRD confirmed the amorphous nature of CDs. Nitrogen doping was confirmed by EDS elemental composition and mapping analysis. FESEM and HRTEM images showed quasi-spherical morphology with irregular size and distribution. Also, the results from HRTEM confined the amorphous nature of as-synthesized NCDs as there was no distinguishable lattice structure. The synthesized NCDs presented appealing concentration, excitation wavelength and pH dependent fluorescence behaviour over the wide range of spectrum. Under all optimized parameters, NCDs could be utilized to analyse Cr (VI) ions with extreme sensitivity and selectivity in an aqueous phase. The as developed “turn-off” fluorescent sensor worked on the principle of static fluorescence quenching mechanism resulted from charge transfer phenomenon from NCDs to chromium ions. The calibration curve form Stern-Volmer plot possessed the linear relationship (R2 = 0.9972) between ratio of intensities (I0/I) versus the quencher concentration (Cr6+) in the range of 0–4 μM with 2.598 nM LOD and the value of Ksv was estimated to be 3.29 × 105 M−1.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to TEQIP-III grant of Dr. SSB University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology and SAIF, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India for providing the instrumental characterizations.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile bulk production of highly blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of mercuric and iodide ions in aqueous media
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Barman, M. Sadhukhan, Facile bulk production of highly blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of mercuric and iodide ions in aqueous media, J. Mater. Chem. 22 (2012) 21832- 21837.

Sens. Actuators, B

RSC Adv.

Chem. Res. Toxicol.

Sci. Total Environ.

Sens. Actuators, B
